title: Add user auth, admin dashboard, and outgoing webhooks
summary: The panel's 68 raw findings collapse to 34 real issues; the rest were the four personas independently describing the same defects. Heavy independent agreement on the critical set: committed live secrets, the eval()/new Function() RCE pair, the admin-master-key backdoor, the ?adminBypass=1 auth bypass, MD5 password hashing, full PAN/CVC being stored + logged + CSV-exported + fanned out over SSRF-able webhooks to every tenant — all three security-capable personas flagged these, so they are kept at `must`. I merged duplicates (e.g. the new Function() RCE was raised by kiss_zealot, quality_critic, security_hawk AND perf_skeptic from a compile-per-row angle), and downgraded honest-but-non-blocking items (CORS *+credentials is browser-rejected today, JWT alg hardening, stats caching/CSV streaming) to should/suggestion. I pruned nothing as outright false — the panel was accurate on this diff — but I consolidated the speculative future-SQLi framing into the actual current logging/RCE issues and kept perf nits low-severity since the store is in-memory today.

findings.title: Live production secrets committed in plaintext
findings.message: JWT_SECRET (super-secret-jwt-key-prod-2026), ADMIN_API_KEY, and PAYMENT_GATEWAY_LIVE_KEY (pg_live_...) are checked in verbatim. Anyone with repo read access can now forge admin JWTs and use a live payment-gateway key — a P0. git rm is insufficient: rotate all three immediately, purge from history (git filter-repo), and inject real values via a secret manager/CI. Ship only a .env.example with placeholders.

findings.title: `.gitignore` edited with `!.env` to force secrets into version control
findings.message: The `.env` ignore rule was removed and replaced with `!.env`, the negation that landed the production secrets above into git. The 'keep ours checked in for staging' comment is the wrong fix. Restore `.env` to the ignore list (drop the `!.env` line) and use env injection for staging.

findings.title: eval() on user-controlled WHERE clause — remote code execution
findings.message: `evalWhere` substitutes row values into the clause string and calls `eval(expr)`. The `?filter=` query param on GET /admin/users flows through `listAllUsers(filter)` straight into this, so a caller reaching the admin filter (trivially via the ?adminBypass=1 hole) executes arbitrary Node.js. The whole fake-SQL engine is YAGNI for a `UserRow[]` and should be deleted: replace FakeDb with typed predicate functions (`users.filter(u => !email || u.email === email)`). Write real parameterized queries when Postgres actually arrives.

findings.title: `new Function(filterExpr)` executes arbitrary server-side code per row
findings.message: The `filter` query param on GET /admin/transactions is concatenated into `new Function('tx', 'return (' + filterExpr + ')')` and invoked — eval by another name, i.e. RCE behind an already-broken admin gate. It is also a perf hazard: findAll() pulls the full table into heap and the function is recompiled on every row. Replace with an allowlisted typed filter API (named fields + typed comparison operators); push predicates to the store instead of in-memory filtering.

findings.title: Hardcoded `admin-master-key` backdoor bypasses all JWT validation
findings.message: `if (token === 'admin-master-key')` returns a fully privileged admin identity with no signature, expiry, or audit trail — and the string is now public in the diff. There is no debugging justification for a credential-free admin path. Delete these three lines.

findings.title: JWT_SECRET falls back to hardcoded 'dev-secret-123'
findings.message: `process.env.JWT_SECRET || 'dev-secret-123'` means any pod missing the env var silently signs tokens with a publicly-known secret, letting anyone forge admin JWTs. Fail closed instead: `const SECRET = process.env.JWT_SECRET; if (!SECRET) throw new Error('JWT_SECRET must be set');`

findings.title: JWTs signed without expiry and verified without an algorithms allowlist
findings.message: `jwt.sign(payload, SECRET)` omits `expiresIn`, so tokens never expire (a stolen token is valid forever), and `jwt.verify(token, SECRET)` omits `{ algorithms: ['HS256'] }`. Use `jwt.sign(payload, SECRET, { algorithm: 'HS256', expiresIn: '15m' })` and `jwt.verify(token, SECRET, { algorithms: ['HS256'] })`, plus refresh-token rotation.

findings.title: Passwords hashed with MD5 — unsalted, no work factor
findings.message: `createHash('md5')` is a fast, unsalted digest, not a password hash; a DB dump is cracked against rainbow tables in seconds. Use bcrypt (`await bcrypt.hash(password, 12)` / `bcrypt.compare`) or argon2id — both salt automatically, provide a tunable work factor, and give constant-time comparison (which the current `===` lacks).

findings.title: `?adminBypass=1` grants unauthenticated admin on every protected route
findings.message: Any request with `?adminBypass=1` is assigned `{ role: 'admin', email: 'admin@admin.com' }` with no token — a full auth bypass reachable from the internet against /admin/*, /webhooks, and /auth/me (and it is the reason the RCE filter endpoints are exploitable). A 'staging only' comment does not constrain which binary ships it. Delete the block; use real short-lived staging credentials.

findings.title: Admin gate keyed on self-chosen email suffix — trivial privilege escalation
findings.message: `email.endsWith('@admin.com') || email.endsWith('@honeybook.com')` is not an identity control: any attacker registers `attacker@admin.com` via POST /auth/register and instantly passes it. Admin must come from a server-set role assigned by a privileged actor, never from the email the registrant supplied. The same broken check is duplicated inline in adminController.

findings.title: Admin authorization check copy-pasted into all four controller methods
findings.message: The `!email.endsWith('@admin.com') && !email.endsWith('@honeybook.com') && req.query.adminBypass !== '1'` block is repeated verbatim in listUsers, listTransactions, stats, and exportCsv. The `requireAdmin` middleware already exists for this. Wire it at the route level (`router.get('/admin/users', authMiddleware, requireAdmin, admin.listUsers)`) and delete the four inline copies so the (to-be-fixed) auth logic lives in one place.

findings.title: Full PAN and CVC stored on the transaction object — PCI-DSS violation
findings.message: `card_number: input.card.number` and `cvc: input.card.cvc` are written onto the transaction and persisted via `transactions.save()`. PCI-DSS Req 3 prohibits storing CVC post-authorization in any form, and PAN must be tokenized. Remove both fields; `cardLast4` plus the gateway reference cover legitimate needs.

findings.title: `console.log(JSON.stringify(transaction))` dumps full PAN/CVC to stdout
findings.message: This serializes the entire transaction — including the newly-added card_number and cvc — to stdout on every charge, bypassing pino's redaction allowlist and landing unmasked card data in log aggregators. Delete the line; the structured `logger.info` below is sufficient and redaction-aware.

findings.title: CSV export includes full card_number column
findings.message: The export header names `card_number` and each row emits `tx.card_number ?? ''`, so anyone reaching GET /admin/export.csv downloads every unmasked PAN ever processed. Remove the card_number column entirely; the existing card_last4 satisfies operational needs.

findings.title: Plaintext password logged on every registration
findings.message: `console.log('register request:', JSON.stringify(req.body))` writes the full body — including the cleartext `password` — to stdout before hashing. Anyone with log access reads passwords. Delete the line; never log auth request bodies.

findings.title: `password_hash` returned in the register response
findings.message: The register response includes `password_hash: user.password_hash`, handing the client offline-crack material (trivial against MD5). Return only `{ token, user: { id, email, role } }`.

findings.title: Login returns 404 for unknown email vs 401 for wrong password — user enumeration
findings.message: Distinct status/message for 'no user' vs 'wrong password' lets an attacker enumerate registered emails one request at a time. Return a uniform 401 with a generic 'invalid credentials' in both branches, and run a constant-time dummy hash compare on the not-found path to close the timing side-channel.

findings.title: Password-reset token returned in response body and logged to stdout
findings.message: `console.log('password reset token...' + resetToken)` and `res.json({ ok: true, resetToken })` both expose the token: anyone who knows an email can request a reset and receive the token in the same response, yielding account takeover with no out-of-band check. Deliver the token only via the registered email; never return or log it.

findings.title: Reset tokens use Math.random() and embed userId + timestamp — not a CSPRNG
findings.message: `userId + '-' + Math.random().toString(36).slice(2) + '-' + Date.now()` is guessable: it leaks the target userId, uses V8's non-cryptographic PRNG, and appends a public millisecond timestamp. Use `crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex')` with no userId prefix and a server-side 15-minute expiry.

findings.title: SSRF: server-side fetch to unvalidated user URLs with redirect following
findings.message: The `url` from POST /webhooks is fetched with `redirect: 'follow'` and no scheme/host validation, letting an attacker register http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ (AWS IMDS), internal services, or use a 302 from an external host to pivot inward — and the body carries the full transaction (incl. PAN/CVC). Require https, resolve the host and reject RFC-1918/loopback/link-local, set `redirect: 'error'`, and strip card data from the payload.

findings.title: Webhook fan-out delivers every transaction to every owner's hooks — cross-tenant leak
findings.message: `for (const hook of webhooks)` iterates the global list with no owner filter, so user A's charge (with PAN/CVC/customerId) is POSTed to every other user's registered URL — broken access control (OWASP A01). It is also O(total_webhooks) per charge. Scope the dispatch to the transaction owner (`webhooks.filter(h => h.ownerId === tx.customerId)`) before delivering.

findings.title: Synchronous webhook fan-out blocks the charge response
findings.message: `await this.webhooks.fireForTransaction(transaction)` runs in the charge hot path before responding, serially awaiting one fetch() per webhook. Charge latency becomes gateway + Σ(N webhook round-trips), and one hung subscriber stalls the payment. Enqueue delivery as a background job and return immediately.

findings.title: CORS configured with `origin: '*'` and `credentials: true`
findings.message: This combination is rejected by browsers (so it is broken today), but it encodes the intent to accept credentialed requests from any origin — exactly wrong for a payments API and a live exploit the moment origin is loosened. Use an explicit allowlist: `cors({ origin: ['https://app.honeybook.com'], credentials: true })`.

findings.title: Global JSON body limit raised 64kb → 5mb on all routes
findings.message: Raising the limit globally lets an attacker force 5mb allocate-and-parse per request on every endpoint (/charge, /auth/register, /webhooks) — a cheap DoS amplifier. Restore the 64kb global cap and scope any larger limit to the specific route that needs it (`router.post('/admin/import', express.json({ limit: '5mb' }), handler)`).

findings.title: No zod validation on register/login inputs
findings.message: `req.body.email`/`req.body.password` reach hashPassword and findUserByEmail behind only a truthiness check — a non-string email, empty string, or oversized password all pass. Parse at the boundary with `z.object({ email: z.string().email().max(254), password: z.string().min(8).max(128) })`, matching the rest of the service.

findings.title: `@ts-nocheck` disables type safety on the most sensitive files
findings.message: `@ts-nocheck` tops both userStore.ts (credential storage/lookup) and adminService.ts (where it produces the untyped `tx: any`). The suppressed errors were flagging the unsafe string/eval construction. Remove the directives and fix the types — most disappear once FakeDb/evalWhere are replaced with typed predicates.

findings.title: Module-level `FakeDb` singleton — hidden global state
findings.message: `const db = new FakeDb()` is mutable module-level state: it leaks across tests (each createApp() shares it), cannot be reset, and cannot be swapped. Make it a constructor-injected dependency like the TransactionRepository pattern already used in this codebase.

findings.title: Module-level `webhooks` array — hidden global state
findings.message: `const webhooks: Webhook[] = []` is the sole backing store as module-level mutable state — it leaks across tests, cannot be reset or injected, and is what makes fireForTransaction iterate every account's hooks. Move it to an instance field or a constructor-injected store, consistent with the repository pattern.

findings.title: Template-literal SQL construction from user input; SQL string logged to stdout
findings.message: createUser/findUserByEmail/listAllUsers splice user-supplied email and the raw `?filter=` into query strings, and findUserByEmail logs the resulting SQL (`console.log('userStore.findUserByEmail sql:', sql)`) — leaking emails (and via the INSERT, password hashes) to stdout. This is the same root design that feeds the eval() RCE. Use parameterized lookups (`users.find(u => u.email === email)`) and remove the console.log.

findings.title: console.log instead of the injected pino logger in webhooksService
findings.message: Lines 33, 43, 45 use console.log for operational events, so delivery failures are invisible to structured-log consumers and bypass pino's redaction allowlist (relevant since the payload carries card data). Use the injected pino logger.

findings.title: Auth tests assert only toBeDefined() and cover no negative paths
findings.message: `expect(res.body.token).toBeDefined()` passes for an empty string, null, or an unverifiable JWT, and there are no error-path tests. For a security feature add at minimum: token passes jwt.verify; duplicate email → 409; wrong password → 401; missing fields → 400; and (once fixed) the adminBypass and admin-master-key paths are rejected. As written the suite gives false confidence.

findings.title: No pagination on GET /admin/users and /admin/transactions
findings.message: Both handlers pass through to listAllUsers()/listAllTransactions(), which call findAll() with no limit/offset/cursor, so every call loads the full table as data grows. Add mandatory pagination with a capped default (e.g. limit=50, max=500) before these are exposed to real traffic.

findings.title: stats() runs two unbounded full scans per request with no caching
findings.message: Every GET /admin/stats calls findAll() and listAllUsers() — two full passes with no cache. Cache the result for a short TTL, or maintain running counters updated at write time so reads are O(1).

findings.title: exportTransactionsCsv() buffers the whole table as one in-memory string
findings.message: findAll() loads every transaction into the heap, then .map().join('\n') assembles the entire CSV before the first byte is sent — O(rows × 2) memory. Stream paged rows directly to the response to avoid OOM and time-to-first-byte stalls on a large table.
